_meta.source: mortgagecalculator.org 'Should I Pay Points?' calculator article, 'Discount Points Example' section.
substitution_note: Bankrate's points calculator (https://www.bankrate.com/mortgages/mortgage-points-calculator/) returns HTTP 404. We substituted with mortgagecalculator.org which publishes a worked example as static HTML.
The page's WITH-POINTS value of $954.83 at 4.0%/30yr/$200,000 is correct (matches engine + npf.pmt + closed-form PMT formula).

The page's NO-POINTS value of $993.10 at 4.5%/30yr/$200,000 is INCORRECT — the correct value is $1,013.37 (verified three independent ways: lib.amortize.build_schedule, numpy_financial.pmt, and the textbook closed-form PMT formula). This appears to be a copy-paste error in the article (possibly originally written for a $195,000 loan amount).

We assert the engine matches the CORRECT no-points value ($1,013.37) and the CORRECT with-points value ($954.83). The oracle's $993.10 is intentionally NOT asserted — see tests/fixtures/oracles/README.md 'Discovered discrepancies' for the full forensic.

The page's claim of '5 years and 9 months' breakeven on $4,000 / $38.27 is also internally inconsistent ($4,000 / $38.27 = 104.5 months, not 69). The engine breakeven against the CORRECT savings ($1013.37 - $954.83 = $58.54/mo) is $4000 / $58.54 = 68.33 → ceil 69 months — which happens to match the oracle's 5yr-9mo claim arithmetically. So the oracle's BREAKEVEN line was correct but inconsistent with the (wrong) no-points P&I.
